q: Electrical energy can be transformed into a wide variety of other useful forms of energy.
The transformation of electrical energy into heat energy is used in many home appliances.
Electrical energy is provided by sources of electromotive force such as batteries and generators.
A steady current will flow through a conductor if a steady potential difference is maintained between its ends.
An electric generator converts chemical energy into electrical energy to provide electrical power.
A battery converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Within a source of electromotive force, positive charges are sent from the negative terminal to the positive terminal.
The main function of an electromotive source is to send negative charges from the positive terminal to the negative terminal within the source.
Electromotive force ($emf$) is defined as the amount of energy provided by the battery per unit charge to move round the complete circuit.
Electromotive force is measured in units of volt ($V$), which is equivalent to joule per coulomb ($J\ C^{-1}$).
Despite its name, electromotive force is actually a physical force.
A source of electromotive force generally does not have any internal resistance.
The internal resistance of a battery depends on its size, chemical properties, life time, and temperature.
The potential difference across the terminals of a battery connected to an external circuit is called the terminal voltage.
Terminal voltage is also referred to as available voltage.
The potential difference between the terminals of a battery is always greater than its $emf$ when used in an electric circuit.
Electromotive force ($emf$) is an open-circuit voltage.
Terminal voltage is a closed-circuit voltage.
Charging a battery means the chemical energy of the battery which has been used up is now supplied back by electrical energy from some external source.
In charging a battery, the potential difference between the terminals is equal to $E + Ir$.
The potential difference between the terminals of a battery in an open circuit is zero.
The total work done in moving a unit positive charge round the complete circuit is $IR + Ir$, which is the $emf$ $E$ of the battery.
Potential difference is the amount of energy required to move a unit charge from one end to another end of a circuit element.
When a battery is discharging, the terminal voltage is $V = E - Ir$.
The circuit equation is given by $I = \frac{E}{R+r}$.
